reasoning: Peer set shows a dark dungeon tileset: near-black foundations, cool gray stone, muted teal/cyan accents, deep crimson fire — heavily atmospheric and desaturated overall. Candidate is a pale beige/tan cobblestone with scattered salmon-pink highlight pixels and light gray blobs. (1) Palette overlap: essentially zero — the warm tan base and pink specks appear nowhere in the peer chromatic range; peers contain no warm sandy tones whatsoever. (2) Pixel density: candidate's cobble blobs are large, low-detail shapes; peers pack considerably more features per tile. (3) Shading technique: both use hard-stepped flat fills, the one consistent point. (4) Warmth/saturation bias: candidate reads warm and light; peers are cold and dark — extreme divergence. (5) Hand-pixeled charm: the random scatter of pink rectangles on the candidate looks algorithmically seeded, not intentionally placed; peers show deliberate hand-crafted pixel decisions. (6) Photo-realism contamination: neither tile has this problem. (7) Overall 'Echoes' feel: the candidate could drop into any generic RPG Maker outdoor pack without notice — it carries no visual DNA linking it to the dark, dense, atmospheric peer family. The pink highlight color is the most damning single artifact: it has no precedent in the peer set and reads as a foreign palette intrusion.
